featured_promo.title: What I Learned at Fair Housing Day 2026 (and Why You Should Care)
featured_promo.body: Harv spent April 14 at UC Berkeley for the 5th Annual C.A.R. Fair Housing Day — seven sessions, three hundred REALTORS in Pauley Ballroom. He rebuilt economist Oscar Wei's 11-slide closing deck as interactive charts you can click through in about ten minutes.
featured_promo.quote: Black first-time buyers in California were 4 percent in 2010. In 2025, they are still 4 percent. Fifteen years of attention, zero movement at the entry point.
featured_promo.quote_attribution: — Oscar Wei, Deputy Chief Economist, C.A.R.

stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 14th.
stocks.news:
Markets pushed higher across the board, with NASDAQ leading at nearly 2%. Big bank earnings stole the show — JPMorgan posted its best quarter ever with over $50 billion in revenue, Citi beat on banking fees, but Wells Fargo missed as its lending-heavy revenue mix weighed on results.

Dell and HP briefly surged on a rumor that Nvidia was eyeing a major PC company acquisition. Nvidia killed the story fast — calling it "completely false" — but the chip giant's own stock has now climbed over 18% in the last two weeks.

economy.commentary:
Citadel CEO Ken Griffin warned the world could be staring down a recession if the Strait of Hormuz stays closed for 6-12 months. Oil has already jumped from $70 to $100 since the conflict started, and Griffin sees the crisis accelerating the shift to clean energy as buyers hunt for alternatives outside oil.

Producer-side inflation surprised to the upside — in a good way. The Producer Price Index rose just 0.5% in March, less than half the 1.1% Wall Street expected. Energy still surged 8.5%, but costs across the rest of the economy held nearly flat — a quiet sign that inflation pressure may be more contained than the headlines suggest.

crypto.commentary:
Crypto ETFs had their best week since January, pulling in $1.1 billion in combined inflows. Bitcoin led with $871M, while Ethereum brought in $196.5M — finally snapping three straight weeks of outflows. Analysts credit the Iran ceasefire and softer inflation data for the renewed appetite.

Crypto exchange Kraken filed for an IPO yesterday, following Coinbase and Gemini onto public markets. Some estimates peg Kraken's valuation at $13 billion — and Deutsche Borse's recent $200 million stake (1.5%) suggests institutional money is already lining up.

real_estate.homebuilder: Rates ticked up just one basis point — both 30 and 15-year fixed barely budged from yesterday as the 10-year yield holds near 4.25%.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 15-city tracker held at 2,560 — 246 coming soon and 211 new listings as the spring market stays in motion.

📍 Fremont: 316 total with 30 new listings and 57 coming soon. Steady spring activity continues.

📍 Oakland: 735 total with 55 new listings — still the Bay's biggest inventory pool by a wide margin.

📍 Hayward: 215 total with 14 new and 12 coming soon.

📍 Livermore: 196 total with 13 new listings hitting the board.

📍 San Ramon: 170 total with 18 new and 23 coming soon — a heavy pipeline forming.

📍 Dublin: 157 total with 14 new listings.

📍 Pleasanton: 124 total with 11 new and 18 coming soon.

📍 Union City: 76 total — still one of the tightest markets on the board.

Tax Day is here — and the IRS is reporting refunds running about 11% higher this year. That extra cash often becomes a down payment, an emergency fund, or a paydown on debt that helps qualify for a mortgage. Worth a conversation with any client who's been on the fence about buying.
